g.data-narration: Welcome to Pointer Concepts in C. Pointers are fundamental to memory management and efficient programming.
A pointer is a variable that stores a memory address instead of a data value. It points to data location.
g.text: A pointer is a variable that stores a memory address of another variable.
Instead of holding data directly, it "points to" where the data is stored in memory.

g.data-narration: Pointers enable efficient array manipulation, dynamic memory allocation, and implementation of complex data structures.
Pointers are declared with an asterisk between the data type and pointer name, specifying what type they point to.
g.text: data_type *pointer_name; — The asterisk makes it a pointer type

g.data-narration: The ampersand (&) operator returns the memory address of a variable, which can be stored in a pointer.
g.text: Returns the memory address of a variable using the & operator.

g.data-narration: The asterisk (*) dereferences a pointer, accessing the value stored at the memory address it points to.
g.text: Accesses the value stored at the address pointed to by the pointer.

g.data-narration: Pointer arithmetic moves the pointer by the size of the data type. Adding 1 to an int pointer moves 4 bytes.
g.text: Adding/subtracting moves by data type size, not bytes. ptr++ moves to next element.

g.data-narration: Array names are constant pointers to the first element. arr[i] is equivalent to *(arr + i).
g.text: Array name is a constant pointer to first element. arr[i] ≡ *(arr + i)

g.data-narration: Pointers enable pass-by-reference, allowing functions to modify caller's variables directly.
g.text: Enable pass-by-reference: functions can modify caller's variables directly.

g.data-narration: Character pointers efficiently traverse strings by incrementing through each character until the null terminator.
g.text: Character pointers traverse strings efficiently by accessing each character until the null terminator.

g.data-narration: A pointer to a pointer (double indirection) stores the address of another pointer, useful for dynamic arrays.
g.text: Double indirection stores address of another pointer. Used for dynamic 2D arrays and advanced structures.

g.data-narration: malloc allocates uninitialized memory blocks of specified size. Always check for NULL before using allocated memory.
g.text: malloc allocates uninitialized memory; calloc allocates zero-initialized memory.

g.data-narration: realloc resizes existing memory blocks while preserving data. free releases memory back to the system.
g.text: realloc resizes memory; free releases it. Always match allocation with deallocation!

g.data-narration: Three common pointer errors: dangling pointers (use-after-free), memory leaks (unfreed memory), and uninitialized pointers.
g.text: Three critical errors to avoid: dangling, memory leaks, and uninitialized pointers.
Set pointer to NULL after free()

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: pointers store addresses, enable dynamic memory, and are essential for efficient C programming.
